summary:
Chogha Golan is an early Neolithic site in the Ilam Province of western Iran, in the Mehran district of the Zagros Mountains piedmont, with occupation levels dated to approximately 11,800–9,800 BCE — making it one of the earliest known agricultural sites in the Zagros region and one of the oldest in the world. Excavations beginning in 2009 by an Iranian-German team produced significant evidence for the intensive use of wild plants alongside the earliest stages of cultivation.

The botanical remains from Chogha Golan include wild emmer wheat, lentils, and other plant species in large quantities, alongside small numbers of specimens with morphological characteristics indicative of early cultivation. The site predates most established PPNA villages in the Levant in its earliest levels, placing the beginnings of plant exploitation and early cultivation in the Zagros at a very early date. Chogha Golan documents an independent or semi-independent trajectory of agricultural origins in the eastern Fertile Crescent, contributing to a picture in which the Neolithic transition was a geographically broad process occurring across multiple regions rather than emanating from a single center. Alongside [[event:1357|Ali Kosh]] and [[event:1355|goat domestication sites]] in the Zagros, Chogha Golan is central to understanding the eastern dimension of the agricultural revolution.
